title: ADHD and Dyslexia Together: How to Study When Reading Is a Double Struggle
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You sit down to read a chapter for your history class. The text is dense. The paragraphs are long. Your ADHD brain struggles to stay focused on the material. Your dyslexic brain struggles to decode the words. Each condition compounds the other. You lose your place. You reread the same sentence three times. You finally finish a paragraph and realize you absorbed nothing. The combination of ADHD and dyslexia turns reading assignments into an exhausting gauntlet that can take three times longer than it takes your classmates. The standard advice for reading comprehension assumes a neurotypical brain. You need strategies designed for the double challenge. Here is how to work with both conditions rather than fighting against them.

Why the Combination Is Harder Than Either Alone

ADHD and dyslexia are separate conditions, but they interact in ways that intensify each other. Dyslexia makes decoding text slow and effortful. ADHD makes sustained attention difficult. Together, they create a situation where reading requires maximum cognitive effort for a brain that struggles to maintain effort on any task that is not intrinsically stimulating. The effort of decoding drains the cognitive resources you need for comprehension. The attention lapses mean you lose context and have to reread. The rereading requires more decoding, which drains more resources. The cycle feeds itself.

The emotional toll is also compounded. Dyslexia often creates anxiety around reading that began in early childhood. ADHD often creates shame around focus and productivity. When a reading assignment triggers both, the emotional response can be overwhelming. You are not just struggling with the text. You are struggling with years of accumulated negative experiences with reading. The anxiety activates your threat response, which further impairs the prefrontal cortex functions you need for comprehension and focus.

Understanding this interaction is not an excuse for avoiding reading. It is a framework for designing strategies that address both conditions simultaneously rather than treating them as separate problems.

Strategy One: Pre-Load the Structure

Do not start reading from the first word. Your brain needs a framework before it engages with details. Spend five minutes previewing the chapter. Read the headings. Look at the diagrams and captions. Read the summary at the end of the chapter if there is one. Read the discussion questions. Your goal is to build a mental outline of what the chapter is going to teach you before you try to absorb the details.

This preview serves both conditions. For your dyslexic brain, it reduces the decoding load because you have already seen the key terms and names. When you encounter them in the text, your brain recognizes them rather than processing them as novel. For your ADHD brain, the preview provides a structure that helps maintain attention. When your focus drifts and returns, the headings and outline help you locate where you are. The framework is a safety net for wandering attention.

Strategy Two: Use Audiobooks and Text-to-Speech

Reading with your eyes is only one way to access text. For the ADHD-dyslexic combination, audio can be transformative. Listening bypasses the decoding barrier of dyslexia entirely. The cognitive resources that would have been spent on word recognition are freed up for comprehension. For ADHD, combining audio with a low-demand physical activity can help sustain attention. Listen while walking, folding laundry, or doing dishes. The physical movement occupies the restless part of your brain while the audio feeds the language centers.

If an audiobook is not available, use text-to-speech software. Most devices have built-in screen readers. Speechify, NaturalReader, and Voice Dream Reader are apps designed for this purpose. Adjust the reading speed. Many dyslexic-ADHD students find that slightly faster than normal speech helps maintain attention by reducing the gaps where the mind wanders. Experiment with speed. Find the pace that keeps you engaged without overwhelming your comprehension.

The ideal workflow is often combined. Listen to the audio while following along visually with the text. The dual input reinforces comprehension. The audio keeps you moving forward when your eyes get stuck. The text keeps you grounded when your attention drifts from the audio.

Strategy Three: Break Reading Into Micro-Sessions

Neurotypical study advice often recommends reading in focused blocks of forty-five minutes or more. For the ADHD-dyslexic brain, that is unrealistic and demoralizing. Break your reading into much smaller sessions. Ten minutes. Five minutes if necessary. Read for a micro-session, then pause. Close your eyes for thirty seconds. Walk around the room. The break resets your attention and gives your brain a moment to recover from the cognitive effort of decoding.

After each micro-session, test yourself. What did you just read? Can you summarize it in one sentence? This retrieval practice serves two purposes. It reinforces the learning. And it gives you immediate feedback on whether you were actually absorbing the material or just moving your eyes across the page. If you cannot summarize the section, your brain checked out at some point. Reread it with fresh attention rather than pressing forward and hoping the gaps will fill themselves in later.

Strategy Four: Reduce Visual Friction

The visual presentation of text affects dyslexic readers significantly. Some fonts, spacing, and color combinations make reading easier. Some make it much harder. If you are reading digitally, adjust the display. Use a dyslexia-friendly font like OpenDyslexic or Lexend. These fonts are designed with heavier bottoms and wider spacing that reduce letter swapping and jumping. Increase the font size. Increase the line spacing. Reduce the column width so each line contains fewer words. These adjustments reduce the visual crowding that contributes to dyslexic reading errors.

If you are reading from a physical book or a PDF that cannot be adjusted, use a physical guide. A bookmark or a ruler placed under the line you are reading. This simple tool serves both conditions. It reduces the visual tracking burden for dyslexia. And it provides a physical anchor for ADHD attention, keeping your eyes on the correct line when your brain wants to wander.

The Bottom Line

ADHD and dyslexia together make reading a double struggle, but the right strategies can reduce the friction significantly. Preview the chapter structure before you read. Use audio to bypass the decoding barrier. Read in micro-sessions with retrieval checks between them. Adjust the visual format to reduce cognitive load. The goal is not to read the way neurotypical students read. The goal is to find your own path through the material, one that works with both of your brains rather than against them.
